section.title: The Sun Stands Still
section.sectionRationale: Israel's treaty with Gibeon draws it into war. Five Amorite kings attack Gibeon, and Israel marches all night to defend its new ally. God routs the enemy, hurls hailstones from heaven, and answers Joshua's astonishing prayer by making the sun stand still. The five kings are captured and executed, and the whole southern region falls in a single campaign. It is one movement, the southern conquest crowned by the day the sun stood still, in which the LORD Himself fights for Israel.
section.thesis: The conquest of the south is unmistakably the LORD's doing: He throws the enemy into panic, kills more by hailstones than by swords, halts sun and moon at the prayer of a man, and gives all the land in one campaign, so that the glory belongs to the God who fights for His people, not to Israel's armies.
section.frameNote: Five beats: the coalition attacks Gibeon and Israel marches to help, verses 1 to 9; the LORD routs the enemy with hail, verses 10 to 11; the sun stands still at Joshua's prayer, verses 12 to 15; the five kings captured and executed, verses 16 to 27; and the southern cities conquered in one campaign, verses 28 to 43.

units.label: The coalition attacks Gibeon and Israel marches to help
units.note: do not be afraid; I have delivered them into your hand

verses.text: Now Adoni-zedek king of Jerusalem heard that Joshua had captured Ai and devoted it to destruction, doing to Ai and its king as he had done to Jericho and its king, and that the people of Gibeon had made peace with Israel and were living near them.
So Adoni-zedek and his people were greatly alarmed, because Gibeon was a great city, like one of the royal cities; it was larger than Ai, and all its men were mighty.
Therefore Adoni-zedek king of Jerusalem sent word to Hoham king of Hebron, Piram king of Jarmuth, Japhia king of Lachish, and Debir king of Eglon, saying,
“Come up and help me. We will attack Gibeon, because they have made peace with Joshua and the Israelites.”
So the five kings of the Amorites, the kings of Jerusalem, Hebron, Jarmuth, Lachish, and Eglon, joined forces and advanced with all their armies. They camped before Gibeon and made war against it.

Then the men of Gibeon sent word to Joshua in the camp at Gilgal: “Do not abandon your servants. Come quickly and save us! Help us, because all the kings of the Amorites from the hill country have joined forces against us.”
So Joshua and his whole army, including all the mighty men of valor, came from Gilgal.
The LORD said to Joshua, “Do not be afraid of them, for I have delivered them into your hand. Not one of them shall stand against you.”
After marching all night from Gilgal, Joshua caught them by surprise.

units.label: The LORD routs the enemy with hail
units.note: more were killed by the hailstones than by the sword

verses.text: And the LORD threw them into {{hamam|confusion}} before Israel, who defeated them in a great slaughter at Gibeon, pursued them along the ascent to Beth-horon, and struck them down as far as Azekah and Makkedah.

As they fled before Israel along the descent from Beth-horon to Azekah, the LORD cast down on them large hailstones from the sky, and more of them were killed by the hailstones than by the swords of the Israelites.

units.label: The sun stands still at Joshua's prayer
units.note: there has been no day like it, for the LORD fought for Israel

verses.text: On the day that the LORD gave the Amorites over to the Israelites, Joshua spoke to the LORD in the presence of Israel: “O sun, stand still over Gibeon, O moon, over the Valley of Aijalon.”

So the sun {{damam|stood still}} and the moon stopped until the nation took vengeance upon its enemies. Is this not written in the Book of Jashar? “So the sun stopped in the middle of the sky and delayed going down about a full day.”
There has been no day like it before or since, when the LORD listened to the voice of a man, because the LORD fought for Israel.
Then Joshua returned with all Israel to the camp at Gilgal.

units.label: The five kings captured and executed
units.note: put your feet on the necks of these kings

verses.text: Now the five kings had fled and hidden in the cave at Makkedah.
And Joshua was informed: “The five kings have been found; they are hiding in the cave at Makkedah.”
So Joshua said, “Roll large stones against the mouth of the cave, and post men there to guard them.
But you, do not stop there. Pursue your enemies and attack them from behind. Do not let them reach their cities, for the LORD your God has delivered them into your hand.”
So Joshua and the Israelites continued to inflict a terrible slaughter until they had finished them off, and the remaining survivors retreated to the fortified cities.
The whole army returned safely to Joshua in the camp at Makkedah, and no one dared to utter a word against the Israelites.
Then Joshua said, “Open the mouth of the cave and bring those five kings out to me.”
So they brought the five kings out of the cave, the kings of Jerusalem, Hebron, Jarmuth, Lachish, and Eglon.

When they had brought the kings to Joshua, he summoned all the men of Israel and said to the army commanders who had accompanied him, “Come here and put your feet on the necks of these kings.” So the commanders came forward and put their feet on their necks.
“Do not be afraid or discouraged,” Joshua said. “Be strong and courageous, for the LORD will do this to all the enemies you fight.”
After this, Joshua struck down and killed the kings, and he hung their bodies on five trees and left them there until evening.

At sunset Joshua ordered that they be taken down from the trees and thrown into the cave in which they had hidden. Then large stones were placed against the mouth of the cave, and the stones are there to this day.

units.label: The southern cities conquered in one campaign

verses.text: On that day Joshua captured Makkedah and put it to the sword, along with its king. He devoted to destruction everyone in the city, leaving no survivors. So he did to the king of Makkedah as he had done to the king of Jericho.
Then Joshua and all Israel with him moved on from Makkedah to Libnah and fought against Libnah.

And the LORD also delivered that city and its king into the hand of Israel, and Joshua put all the people to the sword, leaving no survivors. And he did to the king of Libnah as he had done to the king of Jericho.
And Joshua and all Israel with him moved on from Libnah to Lachish. They laid siege to it and fought against it.
And the LORD delivered Lachish into the hand of Israel, and Joshua captured it on the second day. He put all the people to the sword, just as he had done to Libnah.
At that time Horam king of Gezer went to help Lachish, but Joshua struck him down along with his people, leaving no survivors.
So Joshua moved on from Lachish to Eglon, and all Israel with him. They laid siege to it and fought against it.
That day they captured Eglon and put it to the sword, and Joshua devoted to destruction everyone in the city, just as he had done to Lachish.
Then Joshua and all Israel with him went up from Eglon to Hebron and fought against it.
They captured it and put to the sword its king, all its villages, and all the people. Joshua left no survivors, just as he had done at Eglon; he devoted to destruction Hebron and everyone in it.
Finally Joshua and all Israel with him turned toward Debir and fought against it.

And they captured Debir, its king, and all its villages. They put them to the sword and devoted to destruction everyone in the city, leaving no survivors. Joshua did to Debir and its king as he had done to Hebron and as he had done to Libnah and its king.

So Joshua conquered the whole region, the hill country, the Negev, the foothills, and the slopes, together with all their kings, leaving no survivors. He {{cherem|devoted to destruction}} everything that breathed, just as the LORD, the God of Israel, had commanded.
Joshua conquered the area from Kadesh-barnea to Gaza, and the whole region of Goshen as far as Gibeon.
And because the LORD, the God of Israel, {{lacham|fought}} for Israel, Joshua captured all these kings and their land in one campaign.
Then Joshua returned with all Israel to the camp at Gilgal.

hamam.gloss: To throw into confusion, panic, or rout. The LORD Himself hamam the Amorite armies, the same word used of His routing Egypt at the sea and Sisera's host. The decisive blow is not Israel's night march but God sowing panic among the enemy; the victory is His before a sword falls, and Israel merely pursues a foe God has already broken.

damam.gloss: To be still, to stand silent or motionless, to cease. At Joshua's word the sun damam, stood still, and the moon stopped, so that the day of battle was extended. However one understands the mechanics, the text presents it as an unrepeatable wonder in answer to prayer, evidence that the God who fights for Israel commands even the sun and moon, the very lights the Canaanites worshiped.

cherem.gloss: That which is devoted irrevocably to the LORD, here by destruction. City after city of the south is placed under the cherem as God's judgment on Canaan's entrenched wickedness, carried out just as the LORD commanded. The repeated ban marks the conquest as an act of divine justice under God's authority, not human aggression or greed.

lacham.gloss: To fight, do battle, wage war. The chapter's refrain is that the LORD lacham for Israel; He is the true warrior, and Joshua's armies win because God fights on their behalf. This is the theological heart of the whole conquest: the land is taken not by Israel's might but by the LORD who fights for His people and gives them the victory.

basic.context: Because Gibeon made peace with Israel, five Amorite kings unite to punish it, and Gibeon calls Israel for help. Joshua marches his army all night and takes the enemy by surprise. Then God takes over the battle: He throws the enemy into panic, kills more of them with giant hailstones than Israel kills with the sword, and, in answer to Joshua's bold prayer, makes the sun stand still so the day of victory is extended. The narrator says there was never a day like it, because the LORD fought for Israel. The five kings are captured, executed, and Joshua tells his commanders to put their feet on their necks as a sign of total victory. Then, city by city, the whole south falls in a single campaign. The question underneath is: who really wins Israel's battles?
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative, and its refrain interprets it: the LORD fought for Israel. The famous sun standing still is reported as a genuine, unrepeatable wonder, and Christians debate its mechanics without doubting God's power over creation. The chapter also intensifies the herem, the devoting of cities to destruction, which must be read honestly as God's judgment on Canaan. We do not draw rules for warfare from it; we learn that the victory belongs to God.
basic.originalLanguages: The LORD hamam, throws the enemy into panic; the sun damam, stands still, at Joshua's prayer; city after city is placed under the cherem, devoted to destruction; and the refrain is that the LORD lacham, fought, for Israel.
basic.typology: God fighting for His people and subduing kings points to Christ, who fights the true battle and puts all His enemies under His feet. The feet on the necks of the kings foreshadows the final subjection of every enemy beneath the feet of Christ and His people. The victory won not by human strength pictures the salvation accomplished by God alone.
basic.christ: The LORD who fights for Israel and crushes the kings foreshadows Christ, who conquers sin, death, and the powers, and under whose feet every enemy will be placed, 1 Corinthians 15:25. Joshua's word to fearful commanders, do not be afraid, be strong, for the LORD will do this to all your enemies, echoes forward to the assurance believers have that Christ has overcome the world.
basic.god: God is the divine warrior who fights for His people, commanding hail from heaven and even halting the sun. He is sovereign over creation, listening to a man's prayer and bending the heavens. And He is the righteous Judge, bringing His long-warned judgment on Canaan through Israel. The victory and the glory are entirely His.
basic.crossReferences: Exodus 14 shows the LORD fighting for Israel at the sea while they are still. Psalm 18 pictures God routing enemies with hail and thunder. 1 Corinthians 15 declares Christ reigning until all enemies are under His feet. And Romans 16 promises God will soon crush Satan under His people's feet.
basic.oneStory: The southern conquest is won not by Israel's armies but by the LORD, who panics the enemy, rains hail, halts the sun, and gives the whole land in one campaign. It is a vivid display of God as the warrior who fights for His people, pointing forward to Christ, who wins the decisive victory and puts every enemy under His feet.

context.literary: Chapter 10 is the climax of the conquest of the south, dominated by the refrain that the LORD fought for Israel, verses 14 and 42, which brackets and interprets the whole account. At its center stands the poetic fragment quoted from the Book of Jashar, the sun standing still, set off as the most extraordinary sign of God's involvement. The narrative then moves quickly through the capture of the five kings and a rapid, formulaic list of conquered cities, each falling as the LORD delivered it, hammering home that every victory is God's gift.
context.historical: Adoni-zedek, king of Jerusalem, whose name means lord of righteousness, leads a coalition of southern Amorite city-states alarmed that mighty Gibeon has defected to Israel. Joshua's forced night march from Gilgal, climbing from the Jordan valley into the hills, achieves surprise. The Beth-horon pass was a natural route where a fleeing army would be exposed to a hailstorm. Placing feet on the necks of defeated kings was a recognized ancient sign of total conquest, depicted in the art of the age. The Book of Jashar was an old collection of Israelite poetry, also cited in 2 Samuel 1, now lost.
context.canonical: The chapter belongs to the biblical theme of the LORD as divine warrior, seen at the Red Sea and sung throughout the Psalms, who fights for His people so the glory is His. The sun standing still becomes a byword for God's unmatched power over creation. The image of enemies subdued under the feet reaches its fulfillment in the New Testament, where Christ reigns until He has put all His enemies under His feet and God crushes Satan under the feet of His people, 1 Corinthians 15:25 and Romans 16:20, so that this ancient battle points forward to the final victory of Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that the conquest of the south was won by the LORD Himself fighting for Israel, through panic, hail, and the halting of the sun, and to record the swift, total victory over the southern kings and cities as God's righteous judgment on Canaan, so that the glory belongs to God and not to Israel's armies.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a unique, God-directed campaign with miraculous interventions and the herem; it prescribes neither holy war nor its methods for any later people. What endures is its theology: the LORD is the divine warrior who fights for and saves His people, He is sovereign over all creation, and His judgments on entrenched evil are real and just.

debates.question: How should the sun standing still be understood?

views.label: A literal cosmic miracle
views.case: The text plainly says the sun stopped in the middle of the sky and delayed going down about a full day, and calls it a day like no other; the God who made the heavens can halt or prolong the day, and the passage means for us to marvel at a genuine, unrepeatable miracle answering Joshua's prayer.

views.label: A real event described in observational, poetic language
views.case: Quoted from a poetic source, the Book of Jashar, the words may describe from the observer's standpoint an extended day, or God granting time and light for victory, without settling the astronomical mechanics; on this reading the wonder is real and God-given, but the language is that of poetry and appearance rather than a technical account.

debates.bearing: Both views affirm the central claim the narrator draws out: the LORD listened to the voice of a man and fought for Israel, so that the God who commands sun and moon is the one who wins the battle, and the point is His power and faithfulness, not a scientific explanation.

debates.question: How can the total destruction of the southern cities, the herem, be reconciled with God's goodness?

views.label: Long-delayed, righteous judgment on ripened sin
views.case: Scripture frames the conquest as God's just judgment on generations of Canaanite wickedness that He patiently endured until it was complete, Genesis 15:16 and Deuteronomy 9:4-5; it is judgment carried out under His explicit command, just as the LORD had commanded, verse 40, not human cruelty or land-greed, and the same God warned Israel it would face like judgment for like sins.

views.label: A bounded episode picturing final judgment
views.case: The herem was a limited command tied to this land and moment, dramatizing the holiness of God and the seriousness of sin and pointing forward to the last judgment; the New Testament makes the church's warfare spiritual, not by the sword, 2 Corinthians 10:4, so the conquest is not a model but a signpost to God's ultimate reckoning with evil, from which mercy, as with Rahab and Gibeon, remained available to those who turned to Him.

debates.bearing: Held honestly together, both affirm that God's judgment on sin is real and just and that His mercy is genuine; the conquest is His righteous act, not a warrant for human violence, and it drives us to the cross where judgment and mercy finally meet.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter's vocabulary keeps pointing to God as the true combatant. When battle is joined the LORD hamam the enemy, throws them into panic and rout, the same word used of His shattering Egypt at the sea and Sisera's army in the days of the judges; the decisive stroke is divine terror, not Israelite valor, so that Israel pursues a foe God has already broken, and more die by God's hail than by Israel's swords. Then at Joshua's astonishing prayer the sun damam, it stood still, and the moon stopped, an unrepeatable wonder that shows the God who fights for Israel to be Lord even of the sun and moon, the very heavenly bodies the Canaanites worshiped. City after city is placed under the cherem, devoted to destruction as God's judgment on Canaan, carried out just as the LORD had commanded, marking the campaign as divine justice under God's authority. And over it all rings the refrain: the LORD lacham for Israel, He fought for them. Panic, stilled sun, devotion to God, the LORD fighting: the words together declare that the conquest of the south is the LORD's victory, given to His people.

typology.type: The LORD fighting for Israel and routing kings
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the divine warrior, who conquers sin, death, and the powers and wins the decisive victory for His people, Colossians 2:15 and Revelation 19:11-16.

typology.type: The commanders' feet on the necks of the defeated kings
typology.fulfillment: Every enemy placed under the feet of Christ, and under the feet of His people in Him, 1 Corinthians 15:25 and Romans 16:20.

typology.type: Victory given not by human strength but by God halting the heavens
typology.fulfillment: Salvation accomplished by God alone, so that no one may boast, Ephesians 2:8-9 and 1 Corinthians 1:29-31.

study.christ: The drumbeat of this chapter, the LORD fought for Israel, points straight to the gospel, where God fights and wins the battle His people never could. Joshua's commanders set their feet on the necks of conquered kings, a picture the New Testament takes up and lifts to its climax: Christ must reign until He has put all His enemies under His feet, and the last enemy to be destroyed is death, 1 Corinthians 15:25-26; the God of peace will soon crush Satan under your feet, Romans 16:20. The One who disarmed the rulers and authorities and made a public spectacle of them, triumphing over them at the cross, Colossians 2:15, is the true and greater warrior whom Joshua's victories foreshadow. And Joshua's word to his fearful commanders, do not be afraid, be strong and courageous, for the LORD will do this to all the enemies you fight, becomes the assurance of every believer: in Christ we are more than conquerors, because the decisive battle is already His.
study.god: This chapter puts the might and majesty of God on full display. He is the divine warrior who fights for His people, so that the outcome never really hung on Israel's night march or numbers; He panicked the enemy, He rained the hail, He gave the kings into their hand. He is sovereign over all creation, so absolutely that He listens to the voice of a man and halts the sun and moon in their courses, a day like no other in history. He is the righteous Judge, bringing on Canaan the judgment He had warned of for generations, carried out under His command. And He is faithful to His promise and His people, defending even the Gibeonites whom Israel had sworn to protect. The repeated crediting of every victory to the LORD strips away all ground for human boasting: the God of Joshua 10 is a God who saves by His own mighty arm, and to whom alone the glory belongs.

crossReferences.note: The LORD will fight for you, and you have only to be silent; the same divine-warrior truth that governs Joshua 10.
God routs His enemies with hailstones and lightning, the pattern seen at Beth-horon.
Christ must reign until He has put all His enemies under His feet, fulfilling the image of feet on the necks of kings.
Christ disarmed the rulers and authorities and triumphed over them at the cross, the true divine-warrior victory.
The God of peace will soon crush Satan under your feet, echoing the subjection of enemies in Joshua 10.

study.oneStory: The conquest of the south is won not by Israel but by the LORD, who throws the enemy into panic, hurls hail from heaven, halts the sun at a man's prayer, and gives the whole region in a single campaign. The refrain the LORD fought for Israel interprets it all: this is the divine warrior saving His people. The feet on the necks of the kings and the victory given by God alone point forward to Christ, who wins the decisive battle and will put every enemy, sin, death, and Satan, under His feet.

quiz.q: Why did the five Amorite kings attack Gibeon?

options.text: Because Gibeon, a great city, had made peace with Israel and defected from the Canaanite side.
options.note: Verses 1 to 5 show the kings alarmed that mighty Gibeon had allied with Israel.

options.text: Because Gibeon had attacked them first.
options.note: Gibeon had not attacked them; the kings moved against it for making peace with Israel.

options.text: Because Gibeon controlled the only water in the region.
options.note: The reason given is Gibeon's peace with Israel, not control of water.

options.text: Because Joshua ordered them to attack Gibeon.
options.note: Joshua defended Gibeon; the enemy kings, not Joshua, launched the attack.

quiz.q: How did God fight for Israel in this battle?

options.text: He threw the enemy into panic, killed many with hailstones, and made the sun stand still.
options.note: Verses 10 to 14 credit the panic, the hail, and the halted sun all to the LORD.

options.text: He sent an angel to fight the kings in single combat.
options.note: God intervened powerfully, but through panic, hail, and the halted sun, not an angel in single combat.

options.text: He gave Israel superior weapons and armor.
options.note: The victory came from God's direct acts, not better equipment.

options.text: He did nothing; Israel won purely by its night march.
options.note: The night march gained surprise, but the text says the LORD fought for Israel with panic, hail, and the sun.

quiz.q: What was extraordinary about the day of this battle?

options.text: At Joshua's prayer the sun stood still, and there was no day like it, for the LORD listened to a man.
options.note: Verses 12 to 14 record the unique day when the sun stood still at Joshua's request.

options.text: It was the shortest day in Israel's history.
options.note: The reverse: the day was lengthened, not shortened, so Israel could complete the victory.

options.text: A total eclipse hid the battle from the enemy.
options.note: There was no eclipse; the sun stood still and prolonged the daylight.

options.text: Nothing unusual happened that day.
options.note: The narrator says there had been no day like it before or since; it was utterly unique.

quiz.q: What did placing feet on the necks of the kings signify?

options.text: Complete victory over the enemy, and assurance that the LORD would do the same to all Israel's foes.
options.note: Verses 24 to 25 make it a sign of total conquest and a spur to courage.

options.text: A ritual of mercy sparing the kings' lives.
options.note: It was not mercy; the kings were then executed. It signified total defeat, not sparing them.

options.text: A way of honoring the defeated kings.
options.note: It dishonored and subdued them; it was a sign of conquest, not honor.

options.text: A superstitious rite with no meaning.
options.note: It was a recognized sign of total victory and an encouragement to trust God for future battles.

quiz.q: How much of the southern region fell, and to whom is the victory credited?

options.text: The whole southern region fell in one campaign, because the LORD fought for Israel.
options.note: Verses 40 to 42 credit the sweeping, single-campaign victory to the LORD fighting for Israel.

options.text: Only Gibeon was saved, and no other cities were taken.
options.note: Far more than Gibeon; the whole south fell, and the credit goes to the LORD.

options.text: The cities fell over many years of gradual warfare.
options.note: The text stresses one campaign, not many years, precisely because God fought for them.

options.text: Israel conquered it all by its own superior strategy.
options.note: The victory is explicitly credited to the LORD who fought for Israel, not to human strategy.

questions: The refrain of the chapter is that the LORD fought for Israel. Where in your life are you trying to fight a battle in your own strength that you need to entrust to the God who fights for His people?
Joshua prayed a bold, almost unthinkable prayer, and God answered. What would it look like for you to bring bolder prayers to the God who commands even the sun and moon?
God defended the Gibeonites because Israel had sworn to protect them, even though the alliance began in failure. How does God's faithfulness to His word encourage you when you have made mistakes?

The commanders put their feet on the necks of the kings as a sign of victory, pointing to Christ under whose feet every enemy will be placed. How does knowing Christ's final victory over sin and death shape your courage today?
The whole south fell in one campaign, and the glory went to God alone. Where are you tempted to take credit for what is really God's doing, and how can you give Him the glory instead?

translationNotes.note: The BSB sets off the clause about doing to Ai as to Jericho with em dashes; these have been rendered as commas here. Devoted it to destruction renders a form of the Hebrew cherem, recurring in verses 28, 35, 37, 39, and 40.
The BSB sets off the list of the five kings with em dashes; these have been rendered as commas here, with no change to the wording. The same is done for the lists in verses 23 and 40.

Verses 12 and 13 quote a poetic fragment; the poem lines have been joined in order as running text. The BSB footnote points to the Book of Jashar, an old collection of Israelite poetry now lost, also cited in 2 Samuel 1:18.
Hung their bodies on five trees renders a phrase the BSB footnote also gives as impaled on five poles; the bodies were taken down by evening, as in verse 27 and per Deuteronomy 21:22-23.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Joshua 10:1-43. Prose narrative with a poetic fragment in verses 12 to 13, joined in order as running text. The em dashes in verses 1, 5, 23, and 40 were converted to commas; no em or en dashes remain anywhere in the file. Sermon left as none; no sermon URL was verified this session.
